header.subtitle: Maturity stages of the FP&A function in companies

ui.clickHint: Click a cell for details — description, examples, constraint & graduation

1.desc: Inconsistent, manual, month-end takes more than two weeks; Finance is a back-office function. Reports are produced on request, formats change month to month.
examples: Every manager gets a different Excel
Pack has 40+ pages, no executive summary
CFO builds their own analyses because they don't trust the reports
1.constraint: Excel/manual, no templates. No single source of truth — numbers are challenged in meetings.
1.graduate: Standardize templates, automate the basics. Introduce a reporting calendar, consolidate data sources.

2.short: Monthly packs consistent, but dense and backward-looking.
2.desc: Monthly packs are consistent and on-time, but dense, backward-looking and descriptive rather than explanatory. Finance delivers numbers but no context.
examples: 30-page PDF with P&L, balance sheet, cash flow — without 'so what?'
Board pack and management pack identical
Numbers are correct, but the story is missing
2.constraint: Too long, no insight. Reports answer 'what happened?', not 'what does it mean?'.
2.graduate: Exec-ready packs with max. 10 core pages. KPI highlights on page 1. Commentary with recommended actions.

3.short: Reports tailored to BU goals, stakeholder-specific views.
3.desc: Reports tailored to BU goals. Different stakeholders get different views. Finance uses reports as a communication instrument.
examples: Sales gets pipeline KPIs + margin instead of full-cost P&L
3.constraint: Complex, not linked to strategy. BU reports in silos — no line of sight to corporate strategy.
3.graduate: Couple dashboards to strategy/OKRs. KPI hierarchy: corporate → BU → team.

4.short: KPI dashboards central in leadership. Decision-driving.
4.desc: KPI dashboards central in leadership. C-level uses Finance reports as the primary steering instrument. Reporting drives decisions.
examples: Weekly pulse reports to CEO with 5 KPIs + trend
Board works directly with FP&A dashboards
Integration of financial data + ops data (NPS, tickets, conversion)
4.constraint: Still reactive — alerts trigger analysis, but Finance does not anticipate proactively.
4.graduate: Alerts with automated root-cause pre-analysis. Ops-Finance integrated (xP&A). Narrative automation.

5.desc: Self-service dashboards with proactive insights. Finance delivers decision papers. Reporting is near real-time and democratized.
examples: BU heads run their own ad-hoc analyses without FP&A
Reporting cycle: continuous instead of monthly
5.constraint: Dashboard fatigue. 'Everyone is an analyst' without methodological guidance.
5.graduate: Data governance for self-service. KPI hygiene: kill meetings for redundant reports.

1.desc: Variance commentary is vague and shallow. Analysis = description, no explanatory model.
examples: 'EBITDA below plan due to higher personnel costs' — no detail (HC, salary, bonus?)
1.constraint: Accounting-driven — analysis follows the chart of accounts, not business logic.
1.graduate: Root cause. Driver-based models (Revenue = Users × ARPU × Retention). Waterfall as standard.

2.desc: Consistent variances on M/Q/YTD, but shallow. Focus on budget deviation.
examples: Bridge charts only at P&L level
Analysis purely financial, no ops KPIs
2.constraint: Focus on 'what', not 'why'. Analysis stops at the delta.
2.graduate: Root cause + business input. Price/volume/mix. Ops KPIs in variance analysis.

3.desc: Drivers explained, BU collaboration. Finance asks the right questions, works with BUs on root causes.
examples: Deep dives with BU leads on top-3 variances
3.constraint: Limited influence — heard, not always translated into decisions.
3.graduate: Storytelling + influence training. Analyses → decision papers.

4.short: Prescriptive insights, proactive on opportunities & risks.
4.desc: Prescriptive insights tied to growth levers. Finance proactively identifies opportunities and risks.
4.constraint: Capacity covers requested deep dives, not proactive scanning.
4.graduate: Scenario analysis as a permanent format. Predictive elements. External data systematized.

5.desc: Predictive + prescriptive analytics continuously drive strategy. FP&A = analytical engine.
examples: ML: churn prediction, demand forecasting, pricing
5.graduate: Analytics COE. AI/ML applied selectively. Model governance.

1.short: Annual budget only. No reforecast. 3–4 month process.
1.desc: Annual budget only. Bottom-up over 3–4 months. No mid-year adjustment. Budget = 'contract'.
1.constraint: No reforecast. No mechanism for mid-year adjustment.
1.graduate: Quarterly reforecasts. Accuracy tracking (MAPE, bias). Top-down sanity check.

2.short: Quarterly forecasts, slow and inaccurate (>10% variance).
2.desc: Budget + quarterly forecasts, slow (>2 weeks/cycle), inaccurate (>10% variance). Excel.
examples: Scenarios only ad-hoc for the board
Each BU has its own template
2.graduate: Driver-based models. Standardize templates. Cycle time <1 week. BU owns, FP&A facilitates.

3.short: Rolling forecasts, BU input, partly driver-based.
3.desc: Rolling forecasts, BU input systematized. Partly driver-based. Horizon beyond fiscal year.
examples: BU input on 5–10 drivers instead of 200 accounts
3.constraint: Inconsistent cadence. RF + budget in parallel = double burden.
3.graduate: RF as primary. Reduce level of detail. Budget → annual target.

4.desc: Forecasts steer resource allocation + investments. Scenarios are an integral part.
examples: Capital allocation based on scenarios instead of budget
4.constraint: Ops input not fully integrated. Disconnected systems.
4.graduate: Fully integrated driver-based models (xP&A). Scenario library. AI early-warning.

5.desc: Predictive real-time forecasting. Continuous planning. Forecast = living model.
5.constraint: Tech dependency. Black-box risk. Resistance ('no budget').
5.graduate: AI + human judgment. Model governance. Change management for Beyond Budgeting.

1.short: Finance = budget police. No seat at the table.
1.desc: Finance as budget police and number provider. No seat at the table. BUs see a control function.
examples: Only involved after the decision has been made
1.constraint: No seat at the table. Finance = blocker.
1.graduate: Bi-weekly BU check-ins. Fast ad-hoc. Language: 'possible under conditions'.

2.short: Some BU check-ins, one-way. Reports instead of advice.
2.desc: Check-ins established, communication one-way (Finance → BU). Reports, no advice.
examples: Budget review: Finance presents, BU nods
BU P&L exists, is not understood
2.graduate: BU reporting that BU leads actually want. Joint reviews. Finance learns the BU language.

3.short: Analysts in BU meetings, seat at the table.
3.desc: FP&A embedded in BU meetings, understands ops. Seat at the table, gets consulted.
examples: FP&A in sales meetings with pipeline analyses
3.constraint: Heard, but no vote. Dependent on personal relationships.
3.graduate: Dedicated business partners. Rotations. Finance sign-off >€X.

4.short: Finance proactively shapes strategy. Integrated in leadership.
4.desc: Finance shapes strategy proactively. CFO/Head of FP&A on the leadership team. Initiates analyses.
4.graduate: Cover all BUs. Business-partner role + career path. Enterprise embedding.

5.short: CEO's challenger. Finance indispensable, think tank.
5.desc: Finance indispensable, CEO's challenger. FP&A = strategic think tank.
5.graduate: Rotation program. Enterprise embedding. Culture of constructive challenge.

1.desc: Understaffed, generalists. FP&A not differentiated from accounting. 'People who can do Excel'.
examples: Controller does budget + reporting + consolidation + ad-hoc
Accountant who 'does analyses on the side'
1.constraint: No defined roles. Accounting = FP&A on the org chart.
1.graduate: FP&A as its own function. Role profiles. Hire for analytical profile.

2.short: Own team, but skills focused on reporting.
2.desc: Own team. Analysts + manager. Career paths vague, skills reporting-focused.
2.constraint: Skills focused on reporting. Analytical + communication skills underdeveloped.
2.graduate: Skill matrix. IC vs. management track. Recruit business-savvy talent.

3.short: Mix of builders + partners. Modeling + BU understanding.
3.desc: Builders (analytical) + partners (communicative). Modeling depth + business breadth.
examples: Dedicated roles: analyst, BP, data analyst
Independently delivers investment cases + board decks
3.constraint: Weak pipeline. Poaching. Dependent on 1–2 key people.
3.graduate: Structured career paths. Influence training. Talent branding.

4.desc: Balanced mix, structured progression. FP&A = attractive function. Systematic development.
4.graduate: Retention: comp + dev + visibility. FP&A as a springboard (BU lead, CFO).

5.short: Talent magnet, leadership feeder, CFO pipeline.
5.desc: FP&A = talent magnet + leadership feeder. Attracts top talent, produces leaders.
5.graduate: Culture of influence. Manage the CFO pipeline. FP&A as innovation engine.

1.short: Excel + email only. Data silos, manual, error-prone.
1.desc: Excel + email only. Data in silos, manual. No data model, no BI.
examples: Errors only surface in the board
1.constraint: Errors, slow. 70%+ of time spent on data collection.
1.graduate: Centralize (DWH). BI tool. Single source of truth.

2.short: BI tools available, low adoption. Still Excel-heavy.
2.desc: BI/EPM available, low adoption. Excel remains primary. Data partly central, processes manual.
examples: DWH exists, FP&A falls back to Excel
2.graduate: Champions, training, use cases. SSOT. Eliminate manual steps.

3.short: Tools effective, core processes digital. Media breaks to ops.
3.desc: Tools used effectively, core processes digital. Media breaks to ops. Digital-proficient, not native.
3.constraint: Ops not integrated. Finance and ops data separated.
3.graduate: API integrations. Data model → xP&A. Self-service layer.

4.desc: Finance + ops fully integrated. High automation. Cross-functional analytics. Tech-savvy.
4.constraint: Skills gap. Dependent on tool champions.
4.graduate: Upskilling: SQL, Python, BI modeling. Pilot advanced analytics. Data literacy.

5.desc: AI-enabled, self-service. Analytics COE. Technology = competitive advantage.
5.graduate: Change management. Governance framework. Tech-stack reviews. Tech serves the business.
